items.text: And thus ends all that I doubt I shall ever be able to do with my own eyes in the keeping of my journal.
items.context: Final diary entry, reluctantly concluding his famous diary due to deteriorating eyesight and inability to continue the practice
items.publicReaction: When published as the diary's conclusion, moved readers with its resignation and finality; recognized as historically significant endpoint

items.text: For which God knows my heart I could be as sorry as is possible for one to be for a stranger by whose death he gets L100 per annum.
items.context: On the death of Barlow, from whose position Pepys would benefit financially, showing his complex feelings about the succession
items.publicReaction: When published, illustrated Pepys' frank acknowledgment of mixed emotions regarding professional advancement

items.text: Up; and put on my coloured silk suit very fine, and my new periwigg, bought a good while since, and being first wash'd was this day put on, and I liked it very well.
items.context: Personal entry during the Great Plague documenting his attention to fashion and appearance despite the crisis around him
items.publicReaction: When published, readers noted Pepys' preoccupation with sartorial details even during England's gravest medical crisis

items.text: Pepys proposed a centralised approach to supplying the fleet in 1665, which was accepted.
items.context: During the Second Anglo-Dutch War crisis, Pepys recommended a significant administrative reform for naval victualling that was adopted by the Navy Board
items.publicReaction: Recognized at the time by colleagues as excellent administration; resulted in his promotion and increased salary of £300 annually
items.source: The Diary of Samuel Pepys and historical records of the Navy Board
